title: Show Failed systemd Units
hook: One command tells you which services systemd already knows are broken.
problem: A VPS feels unhealthy, but checking services one by one wastes time and misses failed timers, mounts, and sockets.
starting_state: You are logged into a Linux server using systemd and want a quick failure summary.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints units whose current systemd state is failed.
when_to_use: Use after deploys, reboots, package upgrades, or alerts when you need the fastest systemd health snapshot.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as the only health check; a service can be running but still misconfigured or returning bad responses.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Failed unit rows such as backup.service or app-worker.service.

shorts.title: Find failed systemd services fast.
shorts.voiceover: When a VPS looks sick, do not start by guessing service names. Ask systemd what it already marked failed.
shorts.caption: First server-health check: systemctl --failed.

linkedin.question: What is the first command you run when a Linux server feels unhealthy?
linkedin.cta: Save this for your next VPS incident checklist.

youtube.description: Use systemctl --failed --no-pager to get a read-only list of failed systemd units.

experiments.variant_b: One command shows failed systemd units.

title: Inspect One Service Without Pager Traps
hook: Make systemctl status safe for scripts, screenshots, and quick incident notes.
problem: systemctl status can open a pager, wrap awkwardly, or hide the prompt during a tense server check.
starting_state: You know the service name and need its state plus recent journal lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemd prints the service state, main PID, resource summary, and recent logs.
when_to_use: Use when confirming whether a specific service is active, failed, restarting, or blocked on startup.
when_not_to_use: Do not use when you need long historical logs; use journalctl for a wider time range.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Active state, main PID, memory, and recent service lines.

shorts.title: systemctl status without getting stuck.
shorts.voiceover: For one service, disable the pager and cap log lines. You get useful state without flooding the terminal.
shorts.caption: Use --no-pager and --lines.

linkedin.question: Do you prefer systemctl status or journalctl first when debugging one service?
linkedin.cta: Keep this pattern handy for incident notes and clean screenshots.

youtube.description: A safer service-status pattern: systemctl status nginx --no-pager --lines=30.

title: Read Current-Boot Logs for One Service
hook: Ignore stale logs and inspect only what happened since this boot.
problem: Old service logs can make a current incident look worse or point you at errors from last week.
starting_state: The server uses systemd-journald and you want recent logs for one unit from the current boot only.
what_changed: Nothing changes. journalctl prints the last 80 current-boot log entries for the unit.
when_to_use: Use after a reboot or restart when you only care about the current boot session.
when_not_to_use: Do not use for pre-reboot diagnosis; remove -b or use --since with an exact time range.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent current-boot journal lines for nginx.

shorts.title: Read only this boot's service logs.
shorts.voiceover: When a server has rebooted, stale logs can waste your time. Add -b to journalctl and cap the output.
shorts.caption: Current boot only: journalctl -b.

linkedin.question: How often have old logs sent you down the wrong debugging path?
linkedin.cta: Add -b to your current-boot service checks.

youtube.description: Use journalctl -u SERVICE -b to inspect only logs from the current boot.

title: Check systemd Journal Disk Usage
hook: Before deleting random logs, ask journald how much disk it owns.
problem: Disk alerts often lead people to delete the wrong files without checking whether the systemd journal is the real consumer.
starting_state: A server has limited disk space and systemd-journald is enabled.
what_changed: Nothing changes. journalctl reports the total disk used by archived and active journal files.
when_to_use: Use during disk-space triage before deciding whether journal retention needs adjustment.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to find application log directories outside journald.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Archived and active journals take up a measured amount of disk.

shorts.title: Is journald eating your disk?
shorts.voiceover: Do not delete random log files during a disk alert. First check how much space the systemd journal actually uses.
shorts.caption: Measure before cleanup.

linkedin.question: What is your safest first move during a Linux disk-space alert?
linkedin.cta: Measure before cleanup. This one-liner is a good start.

youtube.description: Check how much disk space systemd-journald is using with journalctl --disk-usage.

title: Find Slow Services During Boot
hook: Find which units made your VPS boot slowly.
problem: A server comes back after reboot, but boot time feels long and there is no obvious culprit.
starting_state: The server has booted under systemd and you want a ranked list of slow startup units.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemd prints units ordered by startup duration.
when_to_use: Use after slow reboots, maintenance windows, or image changes that affect startup time.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume the top line delayed the whole boot; follow up with systemd-analyze critical-chain.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Startup durations sorted from slowest to fastest.

shorts.title: What slowed down boot?
shorts.voiceover: When a VPS takes too long to come back, systemd can rank startup units by duration. Treat it as a lead list.
shorts.caption: Boot triage: systemd-analyze blame.

linkedin.question: Which services usually dominate boot time on your servers?
linkedin.cta: Use this as a lead list, not a final verdict.

youtube.description: Rank systemd units by startup duration with systemd-analyze blame.

title: Check Whether a Service Starts at Boot
hook: Running now does not mean it will survive the next reboot.
problem: A service is manually started during an incident, but nobody verifies whether systemd will start it after reboot.
starting_state: You know the service name and need to confirm boot-time enablement.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints enabled, disabled, static, masked, or another enablement state.
when_to_use: Use after installing services, recovering incidents, or manually starting daemons.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to check whether the service is currently running; use systemctl is-active for that.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.

shorts.title: Will this service start after reboot?
shorts.voiceover: A service can be running right now and still not start on the next reboot. Check enablement separately.
shorts.caption: Pair is-active with is-enabled.

linkedin.question: Have you ever fixed a service only to lose it on reboot?
linkedin.cta: Pair is-active with is-enabled when validating recovery.

youtube.description: Use systemctl is-enabled SERVICE to verify boot-time startup configuration.

experiments.variant_a: Running now is not reboot survival.

title: Check If a Service Is Active
hook: Get a clean yes-or-no service state without the full status page.
problem: Full status output is noisy when a script, checklist, or quick human check only needs the current active state.
starting_state: You know the service name and need the current systemd active state.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemctl prints a compact state such as active, inactive, failed, or activating.
when_to_use: Use in runbooks, smoke checks, and quick recovery validation after restarting a service.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a replacement for endpoint checks, logs, or dependency checks.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.

shorts.title: Quick service state check.
shorts.voiceover: When you only need the current systemd state, skip the full status page. is-active gives a compact answer.
shorts.caption: Fast state check: systemctl is-active.

linkedin.question: What do you pair with systemctl is-active for a real production smoke check?
linkedin.cta: Use this for service state, then verify the user-facing path.

youtube.description: Use systemctl is-active SERVICE for a compact current-state check.

title: Show Recent Server Reboots
hook: Confirm whether the server actually rebooted and when.
problem: After an outage, deploy, or provider event, you need evidence of reboot timing without guessing from logs.
starting_state: The server records login history in wtmp and you need recent reboot events.
what_changed: Nothing changes. last prints recent reboot records from login history.
when_to_use: Use after provider maintenance, unexpected downtime, kernel updates, or suspected crashes.
when_not_to_use: Do not rely on it if wtmp rotation or cleanup removed older records.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent reboot rows with boot time and current running status.

shorts.title: When did this VPS reboot?
shorts.voiceover: If a server was down or restarted, confirm the reboot timeline directly before digging through every service log.
shorts.caption: Reboot evidence: last -x reboot.

linkedin.question: Do you check reboot history during every unexplained outage?
linkedin.cta: Add reboot history to your first five incident commands.

youtube.description: Use last -x reboot to see recent Linux reboot records and build an outage timeline.

title: Check Memory Pressure Quickly
hook: See whether memory is actually tight before restarting services.
problem: A VPS feels slow, and people often restart daemons before checking whether memory pressure is the cause.
starting_state: You are on a Linux server and need a quick memory summary.
what_changed: Nothing changes. free prints total, used, free, shared, buff/cache, and available memory.
when_to_use: Use during slowdowns, deploy validation, memory alerts, or before resizing a VPS.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to identify which process is responsible; follow up with ps, top, or systemd-cgtop.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A memory table with total, used, free, buff/cache, and available columns.

shorts.title: Is your VPS out of memory?
shorts.voiceover: Before restarting services or resizing the server, check memory pressure. On Linux, focus on available memory.
shorts.caption: Memory snapshot: free -h.

linkedin.question: Which memory column do you trust first on Linux: free, used, or available?
linkedin.cta: Teach newer operators to read available before reacting.

youtube.description: Use free -h for a quick Linux memory-pressure snapshot and focus on the available column.

experiments.variant_a: Explain why low free memory can be normal.
experiments.variant_b: Show only the available-memory rule of thumb.

title: List Upcoming systemd Timers
hook: Cron is not the only scheduler on modern Linux servers.
problem: Backups, renewals, and cleanup jobs may run as systemd timers, but they are easy to miss if you only inspect cron.
starting_state: The server uses systemd and you need to see scheduled timer units.
what_changed: Nothing changes. systemd prints timer units, next run time, last run time, and the service each timer activates.
when_to_use: Use when checking backups, certificate renewals, cleanup jobs, or unexpected periodic work.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to inspect classic cron jobs; check crontabs separately.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Timer rows showing NEXT, LEFT, LAST, UNIT, and ACTIVATES.

shorts.title: Find scheduled systemd jobs.
shorts.voiceover: Modern Linux servers often use systemd timers for backups, renewals, and cleanup. If you only check cron, you can miss half the schedule.
shorts.caption: Scheduled work: systemctl list-timers.

linkedin.question: Which scheduled jobs on your servers moved from cron to systemd timers?
linkedin.cta: Add systemd timers to your server audit checklist.

youtube.description: Use systemctl list-timers --all --no-pager to inspect scheduled systemd timer jobs.

experiments.variant_b: Frame around backups and certificate renewals.
